---
title: "Put-Call Parity"
date: "2026-06-05"
categories: [foundations, options]
description: "Deriving the put-call parity relationship and exploring its implications for options pricing and arbitrage."
bibliography: references.bib
nocite: '@*'
---

Put-call parity is one of the most elegant results in options theory. It establishes a precise relationship between the prices of European calls and puts on the same underlying, with the same strike and expiration.

## The Relationship

For European options on a non-dividend-paying stock, put-call parity states:

$$C - P = S_0 - K e^{-rT}$$

where $C$ is the call price, $P$ is the put price, $S_0$ is the current stock price, $K$ is the strike, $r$ is the risk-free rate, and $T$ is the time to expiration.

## Deriving it from No-Arbitrage

The relationship follows directly from the no-arbitrage principle. Consider two portfolios: one holding a call and a bond, the other holding a put and the stock. Both portfolios have identical payoffs at expiration — so by no-arbitrage, they must have the same value today.

## Implications

Put-call parity is model-free — it holds regardless of how we model the underlying. This makes it a powerful tool for detecting mispricing and for synthesising positions synthetically.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

<hr class="revision-divider">
<details>
<summary>Revision History</summary>

- June 2026 — First published

</details>
